---
title: "Exercise: SAXPY — uniforms for scalars"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
---

## Goal

Compute `c[i] = a * x[i] + y[i]` on the GPU — the classic **SAXPY** [*single-precision A·X plus Y; the canonical BLAS level-1 benchmark since the 1970s*] kernel. The scalar `a` is the same for every thread — perfect use case for a **uniform buffer**.

By the time `cargo test` passes you will have used `wgpu::BufferUsages::UNIFORM`, the WGSL `var<uniform>` declaration, and seen how a small constant is broadcast to every thread.

## Background

The shader adds one new binding — a uniform buffer holding the scalar — but the structure is otherwise identical to exercise 1:

```wgsl
struct Scalar {
    a: f32,
}

@group(0) @binding(0) var<uniform>             params: Scalar;
@group(0) @binding(1) var<storage, read>       x:      array<f32>;
@group(0) @binding(2) var<storage, read>       y:      array<f32>;
@group(0) @binding(3) var<storage, read_write> c:      array<f32>;

@compute @workgroup_size(64)
fn main(@builtin(global_invocation_id) gid: vec3<u32>) {
    let i = gid.x;
    if (i >= arrayLength(&c)) { return; }
    c[i] = params.a * x[i] + y[i];
}
```

Two things to notice:

- **Uniform buffers wrap the scalar in a `struct`** — WGSL uniforms must be structs (a quirk of the alignment rules; you can't have a bare `f32` at top-level uniform binding).
- **Same access pattern from every thread** — every thread reads `params.a`. The driver broadcasts the value efficiently; reading a uniform is essentially free compared to a storage read.

## Why a uniform rather than a storage buffer for `a`?

You *could* put `a` in a storage buffer. Two reasons not to:

1. **Speed.** Uniform buffers are kept in a fast on-chip constant cache; storage buffers go through general memory.
2. **Convention.** Anyone reading your code expects "one number, same for every thread" to be a uniform. Reading a storage buffer for a scalar is a code smell.

The size limit on uniform buffers is small (typically 64 KB) — for anything bigger, you have to use a storage buffer anyway.

## The exercise

Open `day12/ex-saxpy/`. The wgpu boilerplate and shader are fully written. Your job:

1. Construct the uniform buffer holding `a` (one `f32`, padded to the alignment WGSL expects).
2. Add the binding for the uniform buffer in the `BindGroup`.

Both TODOs are short. Tests check `c[i] = 2.5 * x[i] + y[i]` on a small batch and on a longer one.

You may **not** change `src/shader.wgsl` or the test code.

## How to run

```bash
cd day12/ex-saxpy
cargo test
cargo run --release
```

## Hints

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 1 — the uniform struct in Rust"}
The shader declares `struct Scalar { a: f32 }`. WGSL requires uniform-struct fields to be 16-byte aligned at the struct boundary, but a single `f32` at offset 0 is fine — you just need to upload enough bytes that the buffer size is at least the struct's `min_binding_size`.

The simplest, portable approach: upload 16 bytes (one `f32` plus 12 bytes of padding):

```rust
#[repr(C)]
#[derive(Clone, Copy, bytemuck::Pod, bytemuck::Zeroable)]
struct Scalar { a: f32, _pad: [f32; 3] }

let scalar = Scalar { a: 2.5, _pad: [0.0; 3] };
let scalar_buf = device.create_buffer_init(&wgpu::util::BufferInitDescriptor {
    label: Some("scalar"),
    contents: bytemuck::bytes_of(&scalar),
    usage: wgpu::BufferUsages::UNIFORM | wgpu::BufferUsages::COPY_DST,
});
```

For tiny single-scalar uniforms, the padding cost is invisible.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 2 — bind group entry for the uniform"}
The bind-group entry uses `binding: 0`, the same `BufferBinding` API as storage buffers, but with a uniform-typed buffer:

```rust
wgpu::BindGroupEntry {
    binding: 0,
    resource: scalar_buf.as_entire_binding(),
}
```

The shader's `@binding(0)` and the entry's `binding: 0` must match. The other entries (`x`, `y`, `c`) get bindings 1, 2, 3.
:::

## Solution

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Solution"}
See `day12/ex-saxpy-solution/src/main.rs`. The uniform buffer is created as in hint 1; the bind-group entry list is `[scalar at 0, x at 1, y at 2, c at 3]`.
:::

## Where this leads

- **Exercise 5 (reduction)** uses a uniform to pass `n` (the input length) into the shader — same pattern, integer instead of float.
- **In Burn**, the equivalent of SAXPY is `a * x + y` — one line. Burn's matmul, conv, attention kernels all pass per-layer constants via uniforms exactly like this.
